narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in this field, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admissions model looks at patterns from past students—like their early and later school grades, LSAT scores, undergraduate GPAs, family income levels, and whether they plan to study full-time or part-time—to predict the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. For you, it estimated a 40% probability of failing the bar exam, which crossed the threshold that triggered the rejection to protect the school's outcomes. Your LSAT score of 35 stood out as your strongest asset, slightly below the typical student's 36.7 but powerfully pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass, as it was the most influential positive factor. On the flip side, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2—well below the average of about 5.5—was the biggest drag, strongly signaling to the model a higher risk of bar failure compared to most applicants who rank higher. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.8, lower than the usual 3.21, also pulled against you but not as forcefully, while your upper-middle family income helped nudge things positively by aligning with profiles of successful students. Choosing part-time study, which is uncommon (only about 7% of students do this versus 93% full-time), added a moderate concern for the model, as it deviates from the typical path. Your Year 1 decile of 1 had a smaller negative pull, showing early grades that were lower than average too. Overall, while some of your strengths like your LSAT and family background compare favorably to typical students, the gaps in your later school performance and part-time plans tipped the scales toward caution. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar hurdles through targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it further, strengthening your recent grades if possible, exploring full-time options, and seeking programs with holistic reviews that value your unique background as a Black woman. You're capable of turning this around; keep pushing, and reach out to advisors for personalized strategies.
